segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a warning about scenes involving addiction and illegal drugs, immediately followed by a montage of illicit drug use: a person in a black balaclava snorts a white powder from a plate while another drinks from a bottle, all set to an upbeat, tense musical score. A female narrator begins, stating, "Cocaine is sweeping through Germany like never before." The scene shifts to a close-up of hands preparing a line of powder with a card, then to a cityscape at dusk with train tracks and the Berlin TV Tower, as the narrator continues, "Getting cocaine in Berlin is as easy as buying cigarettes. Available whenever, wherever, and with enormous profits." A man in a white shirt with a blurred face is seen sitting at a desk in a dimly lit office, surrounded by papers and a calculator, while a male voice, translated from German, explains, "Buying high quality makes you a minimum 100% profit per capsule." This is intercut with nighttime scenes of a customs officer inspecting a vehicle, and a woman with blonde hair and glasses sitting at a desk in a modern office, appearing to speak. The female narrator adds, "Cocaine is an integral part of Germany's party scene. It keeps you awake and puts you in a good mood," before continuing, "International drug gangs run a perfectly organized smuggling network." An accented male voice then gravely warns, "This swallowing system is very risky because when they bust in your stomach, you can die." The narrator emphasizes, "Never before have the drug cartels seemed so powerful," as sounds of shouting and commotion erupt, followed by a male voice urgently commanding, "Show your hands!" A female voice, translated from German, then laments, "We can't control this huge flood of cocaine through checks." The segment ends with a close-up of a line of white powder on a black surface next to a plastic straw, overlaid with the title 'COCAINE FOR GERMANY' and 'Drug Dealing and Smuggling' from DocFilm DW, as the female narrator dramatically asks, "Germany is on cocaine. What can stop the drug crisis?", with the tense music swelling and fading.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a sweeping aerial night view of a cityscape, accompanied by electronic, slightly ominous background music and a distinct 'whoosh' sound effect, as the 'DocFilm DW' title card and 'Drug Dealing and Smuggling' text establish the documentary's theme. A female narrator begins, stating that 'Through the night, cocaine is the stuff of this breathless big city,' and that 'Getting it seems easier than ever. Demand is growing and growing.' The scene transitions to a dimly lit urban street at night, where a group of people are seen standing near a corner, followed by a quick shot of a person in a black balaclava walking through a dark alley. The narrator continues, explaining that 'In this film, we follow the journey of cocaine from cultivation in South America, the smuggling route via Africa and Spain, to the dealers and consumers in Berlin.' The video then shifts to a vibrant, blurred party scene with people dancing under colorful lights, suggesting a nightlife setting, while the rhythmic music continues. A distant view of the Berlin TV Tower at dusk appears, grounding the location. The narrator poses questions: 'We want to know how the business works. What authorities are doing about the boom. And who is profiting,' before asking, 'But why is cocaine so celebrated in Germany?' A distinct 'whoop' sound is heard, and the background music shifts to an upbeat, electronic dance track, as the video cuts to a close-up of a person in a black balaclava snorting white powder from a plate in a dimly lit room, with beer bottles and cards on the table. This is followed by a rooftop scene at sunset where two individuals in black balaclavas stand silhouetted against the city skyline, one smoking a cigarette, creating a tense, clandestine atmosphere. The narrator identifies these as 'heydays for cocaine dealers like these two,' explicitly naming them 'Jonas and Max,' and adding that 'They've been dealing for over six years.' The sequence ends with a close-up of a hand holding a lit cigarette, emphasizing the illicit activities, as the club-like music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a wide shot of a calm urban setting in Berlin, featuring the Reichstag building with its iconic glass dome and German flag, alongside modern architecture and a boat moving along the river, as a female narrator speaks about "work or private problems become too great." The scene transitions to an interior, focusing on Marie, identified by on-screen text as a 'Former cocaine user,' a woman with short blonde hair in a blue shirt, seen from behind in a softly lit room. Melancholic background music begins as a male voice, recounting Marie's past experience, describes how when he started using cocaine, it had a positive, euphoric effect, making him feel alive and strong. The narrator confirms Marie was addicted to cocaine for several years and has only been clean for a few months. Marie is shown in various states of contemplation and activity: sitting on a mustard-yellow sofa with a striped pillow, typing on a black Fujitsu keyboard at a desk, and tending to colorful flowers in balcony boxes. Her hands are shown in close-up, clasped or resting, emphasizing introspection. The narrator explains Marie works as a lobbyist, advising politicians, a stressful job. Her addiction progressed from initial party use to taking the drug for efficiency at work, eventually leading to a loss of control. The male voice continues, detailing the peak of his addiction, needing three needles a day, spending 100 to 150 daily, and shooting up 20 or 30 times a day, including five or six times at work. The balcony scene reveals a cityscape with residential buildings, and Marie's reflection in the glass underscores her solitude and reflective journey post-recovery.

The scene transitions to a laboratory where a person in blue gloves meticulously grinds white crystalline substances in a mortar and transfers them into test tubes for forensic analysis, as a narrator explains that several kilos were seized during a raid and are being analyzed for purity. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with OLAF SCHREMM, Drug Investigation Berlin State Criminal Police Office, seated at his cluttered office desk, speaking directly to the camera in a calm male voice, explaining that the market continues to function unchanged despite many tons being withdrawn from circulation. A car driving sound is heard before tense background music begins, as the scene transitions to a wide shot of the Hamburg port, showcasing massive container ships and towering cranes, while a female narrator emphasizes that the "ever more, ever purer" cocaine supply seems to never tire, and Hamburg is a major gateway where customs intercept growing quantities. The narrative shifts to an indoor setting where two men, one in a suit and the other in a grey 'ZOLL' uniform, both wearing white face masks and blue gloves, carry a large blue container down a sterile hallway. They place the container on a table and begin unpacking it, revealing wrapped packages—some green, some beige—indicating seized contraband. The man in the suit, identified as RENÉ MATSCHKE, Hamburg Customs, speaks in a clear male voice, describing these as "the classic kilo packages of cocaine" from almost all captures, noting they are "well packed and odor-proof," each containing "approx. 1 kg net cocaine." The scene cuts to a ZDF news broadcast clip, where a male anchor reports on a 'Rekordmenge Kokain sichergestellt' (Record amount of cocaine seized), exclaiming "What a success!" and stating that customs seized over 16 tons of cocaine in the port of Hamburg, with visuals of white powder and containers. The video returns to the lab setting, where Matschke and the customs officer continue to handle the packages, now placing them on a blue mat marked with 'ZOLL' tape for forensic documentation. The female narrator concludes, affirming it's a successful and large find for Hamburg Customs, as the background music fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with JONAS, identified by a female narrator as one of two dealers (the other being Max), in a black balaclava and dark hoodie, handling playing cards on a wooden table in a dimly lit apartment. The scene quickly shifts to a rooftop at dusk, with the Berlin TV Tower visible in the background. Here, JONAS speaks directly to the camera in German, translated to English, explaining how he initially didn't want to sell drugs but, after sorting something out for himself, more and more people approached him, leading to a continuous rise in demand. He notes that this is how problems often arise. During his narration, sounds of snorting are heard, and JONAS comments, "Normally not with a mask," implying his disguise is for the camera. The scene cuts back to the apartment where JONAS and a second individual, also in a black balaclava and similar hoodie (implicitly Max), are meticulously preparing substances. Rhythmic crushing sounds accompany the visual of one individual, wearing white gloves, crushing a white crystalline substance on a plate and measuring it on a digital scale. JONAS briefly remarks, "My life," as the preparation continues. Ambient electronic music begins to build tension. The female narrator returns, specifying that JONAS and Max are filling "cocaine capsules" for nightly delivery, revealing they earn approximately 100 euros per gram, depending on quality. The two continue their clandestine work on a couch, surrounded by bookshelves and modern decor, as the music fades slightly.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of JONAS (NAME CHANGED), a Berlin dealer, wearing a black balaclava and a dark hoodie with an 'OFF PATH' graphic, seated indoors in a dimly lit room. He speaks directly to the camera with a serious expression, explaining in German (translated to English) how buying high-quality drugs ensures "100% profit" on a capsule, citing an example of buying a gram for €50 and selling it for €100. Beside him, a second person in a balaclava handles and drinks from a bottle. Upbeat, slightly tense background music begins as the scene shifts to a nighttime urban street, where a person in dark clothing walks briskly. This is followed by a shot of a car’s GPS navigation display showing a route through Berlin, including 'Manteuffelstraße', while a female narrator explains that "Jonas and Max" are "nocturnal drug couriers" delivering products directly to customers' homes, describing it as an "illegal business" yet "quite normal" in the city. The video then cuts to MARIE (NAME CHANGED), a former cocaine user, a blonde woman seen from behind on a balcony, lighting and smoking a cigarette with a cityscape in the background, exhaling smoke as the camera focuses on her hand. The narrator continues, detailing how Marie "needed more and more" cocaine, spending "2,000 euros a month" and losing "all her savings" to the drug. The scene transitions to Marie sitting indoors, wearing a light blue button-up shirt, a small mole visible on her neck, her hand resting on a wooden chair, as she reflects and speaks in German (translated to English) about the moment she realized the extent of her addiction when she couldn't pay rent, prompting her to think of ways to get money quickly.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of MARIE, a former cocaine user, seen from behind in a light blue button-up shirt with a visible mole on her neck, as a female voice (speaking in both English and German) recounts her past struggle, stating, "when it became clear to me that I was at the point that I would prostitute myself to get money for drug use, that was already rock bottom for me. My very personal rock bottom." She continues, expressing that she "didn't know how I could save myself from that." The scene transitions to MARIE in a bathroom, leaning over a white sink and washing her face under running water, a moment of personal care, as the audio shifts to a melancholic background music. A narrator then explains that "Following three years on cocaine, Marie went into withdrawal. Her body reacted with strong fever-like symptoms," requiring her to "regularly cool down with cold water" and undergo "therapy to overcome the mental dependency." MARIE, wearing a clear beaded bracelet, then exits the bathroom and walks outside through a residential area with trees and parked cars, carrying a black shoulder bag. The narrator emphasizes, "Marie wants to make it, but the road out of addiction is long." She enters a building where she is greeted by Hans Peter Eckert, a man in a white shirt and face mask, who the narrator identifies as providing "psychological care to addicts during their withdrawal." They walk together down a hallway lined with framed artwork, passing a frosted glass block window, indicating a structured therapeutic environment where her "treatment plan includes regular sessions at a therapy center."

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens in a calm, clinical or therapeutic room with neutral walls and a glass-block wall, where a man in a white shirt, blue jeans, glasses, and white sneakers, seated in a wooden armchair with blue upholstery, attentively listens to MARIE, a blonde woman in a light blue button-up shirt, also seated in a similar chair. A small wooden side table with two glasses of water on a red coaster sits between them. MARIE, seen from behind with her short blonde hair and a mole on her neck, wearing a clear beaded bracelet, recounts in German, translated as, "Last week, a friend of mine was invited to a birthday and asked me if I would like to go. I did not know the birthday girl or other guests at the celebration." The man, appearing to be a counselor or therapist, then asks in German, translated as, "And you didn't realize that there were any situations or opportunities where something might happen?" MARIE responds, "No, I didn't." The man continues, "And did it shake you? Did you leave as we practiced?" MARIE confirms, "Yes, we practiced that." She elaborates, "When I realized what was happening, I sat there for about 10 minutes. And then I knew that I had to go. I told my friend and she reacted immediately and also left." The man, occasionally shifting his posture and gesturing subtly, then asks, "Did you later regret your decision not to consume?" MARIE firmly replies, "No. And that was a first. Other times I did regret, but not this time." The soft, even lighting contributes to a serene, introspective atmosphere throughout their serious, reflective dialogue, with the 'DW' logo visible in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with a close-up from behind MARIE, a former cocaine user, seated in a blue armchair, her short blonde hair and a mole on her neck visible, as a soft breathing sound is heard. The camera then shifts focus to HANSPETER ECKERT, Therapeutic Manager at Kokon e.V., seated across from her in a wooden armchair, wearing a white shirt and glasses, in a calm, clinical setting. He speaks directly to the camera, initially saying "Cool. Cool." in German, then explaining in German with an English voiceover that "Cocaine is so harmful because it makes you, as the person affected, dependent, such that you can no longer complete tasks that are worthwhile to you, because you want to consume all the time." He continues, stating that "In those times of consumption, most female patients withdraw socially to a large degree and are no longer reachable at all." As Hanspeter speaks, the scene transitions to a series of wide urban shots of Berlin, including its skyline with the iconic TV Tower, a busy city street with traffic and pedestrians, and an aerial view of a dense urban neighborhood. Ominous, sustained synth music begins to play, accompanying a dark, stylized map of Germany with a highlighted region and an arrow pointing to it, credited to 'EBBD'. A female narrator's voice then states, "Once you've become an addict, it's hard. Only about one-third succeed in quitting permanently. All the others relapse. The damage to people and society is enormous." The music continues as the narrator concludes, "From South America, cocaine travels all over the world."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a stylized, dark-toned animated world map, outlined in red, dynamically illustrating trafficking routes with white arrows from South America to Europe and Africa. A female narrator explains that the high-value commodity becomes increasingly impure and expensive on its way to consumers, detailing that its route to Europe is either direct or via West African states like Guinea-Bissau or Nigeria, where deliveries often change hands. Text overlays on the map indicate a starting price of '€3,000 per kilo' near South America, with the narrator specifying this is the price in Colombia, and that dealers in Germany charge more than ten times that. The substance, she adds, is transported by container, ship, air, or even inside a smuggler's body. A slightly tense, rhythmic musical score begins as the scene transitions from the abstract map to real-world footage. An aerial view reveals the bustling Kenyan city of Mombasa, identified by the narrator as having the largest port in East Africa, where a new drug trafficking route has recently developed for heroin from Asia and cocaine from Latin America. The cityscape shows dense buildings, a roundabout with colorful minibuses, and a Stanbic Bank branch, followed by a pan across industrial zones with shipping containers and warehouses, and finally a street-level view of pedestrians and vehicles, including a man selling goods from a crate, emphasizing the city's vibrant daily life and economic activity.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of dark-skinned hands, wearing a white shirt, meticulously handling a small object on a wooden desk. Tense, low synth music begins as a female narrator states the subject is involved "in drugs to Europe and Asia, including cocaine, as a mule." The camera pulls back to reveal IDRISS, a drug smuggler with a blurred face, seated at the wooden desk in a dimly lit office with peeling blue walls. He gestures with his hands, speaking in a calm, instructional tone with a non-native English accent about the process of swallowing a "silicon material" with "salad oil" to transport drugs, emphasizing the need to "swallow slowly by slowly" and avoid eating acidic food. He advises pretending to eat on the plane to avoid suspicion from security. IDRISS then stands and walks to the window, looking out at a dusty, rural landscape with a white truck and piles of sand or gravel. The scene abruptly cuts to an aerial view from an airplane window, showing the wing with a red and green tail fin flying over clouds, followed by a ground-level shot from a vehicle driving on an airport tarmac past parked planes. IDRISS's voice continues, explaining that upon reaching the airport, one must use "back doors" to avoid the "scanning machine" and be taken by a vehicle to a hotel to "remove your stuff." The visual transitions to a modern airport terminal bustling with masked travelers, then returns to IDRISS standing by the window in his office, gazing out at the same dusty scene, concluding his instructions with "The swallowing system."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a blurred IDRISS, a drug smuggler, seated at a wooden desk in a dimly lit office with peeling blue walls, wearing a white 'Trump' shirt. He speaks with a non-native accent, explaining the extreme risks of smuggling, stating, "It's very risky... when they bust in your stomach, you can die," but justifies it by needing money to "support my family." The scene then flashes to a vibrant, overexposed party with a hand adjusting a turntable and people dancing under colorful lights, while a female narrator explains that smuggled cocaine typically ends up in "German clubs via dealers, getting party-goers high." This is underscored by a rhythmic, atmospheric electronic beat. The visuals shift to an aerial view of an African coastal city with industrial buildings and shipping containers, then a ground-level shot of the 'OFFICE OF THE MICA FRERETOWN WARD REPRESENTATIVE', and a person walking down a dusty street, illustrating the "long and extremely dangerous" road mentioned by the narrator. Returning to IDRISS, now standing by a window looking at a rural landscape with a white truck, he then sits back down, clasping his hands. He continues speaking, expressing deep regret for entering the system, "because now I cannot get out," fearing that "the dons can send somebody to come and kidnap your family or kill you" if he leaks information to security agencies, concluding that "it is not moral and correct." The camera focuses on his tense hands and the worn office environment, emphasizing his contemplation and fear.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with IDRISS (NAME CHANGED), a drug smuggler with a blurred face, seated at a wooden desk in a dimly lit office with peeling blue walls. He speaks with a distinct accent, expressing profound regret: "I know I'm affecting a lot of youth through my job. Sometimes I regret, tears come from my eyes, but I cannot do anything. I just live, only God will judge me." He gestures while speaking, then stands to look out a window at a dusty rural landscape before returning to his seat, clasping his hands reflectively. The scene shifts to him washing his face at a sink in a tiled bathroom, accompanied by the sound of running water and splashing, before he returns to the office. A close-up reveals a calendar for November 2020, advertising 'Air, sea and land freight' and 'Customs clearance,' suggesting a front business. A female narrator then states, "Idris knows exactly which routes the large quantities of cocaine take from East Africa to Europe." IDRISS resumes speaking, detailing these routes over an animated dark world map with red continent outlines and white arrows tracing trafficking paths: "To Europe, after reaching South Africa, it is transported to Nigeria. After Nigeria, it goes to Tunisia or Morocco. There they transport using the fishing boat. And there is another route after reaching Tanzania or Kenya, to South and Sudan, Khartoum. After Khartoum, it is transported through the desert using the camel until it reaches Tunisia." The map emphasizes the Strait of Gibraltar, labeled 'Distance: 13km', highlighting the global scale and strategic importance of these operations.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an aerial view of a coastal road stretching toward a prominent mountain, flanked by dry vegetation and a cityscape, establishing a port city. A male voice briefly mentions a route through "Morocco," before a female narrator identifies the location as La Línea de la Concepción in Southern Spain, a city known as a gateway for drug smuggling between Africa and Europe, where the Strait of Gibraltar became a European base for traffickers in the 1970s, initially for hashish and now cocaine. The camera transitions to ground-level shots of a beach with a large industrial port, later specified as Algeciras, in the background, featuring blue cranes and shipping containers, indicating a major logistics hub, accompanied by the distinct sound of a boat engine. Urban streets with palm trees, colorful buildings, and moderate traffic capture the city’s daily life. As dusk falls, city lights glow and the port’s cranes illuminate, emphasizing its operational importance as the primary arrival point for large drug quantities. The final sequence focuses on a man in a dark puffer jacket and cap, seen from behind, sitting in a dimly lit room and gazing out a frosted glass door. This man is revealed to be Mike, a key figure for one of Spain's largest narcotics cartels, responsible for organizing drug logistics to leave the port unnoticed. The camera slowly moves closer, capturing his stillness and the quiet atmosphere, before ending on a close-up of the door’s edge, reinforcing a theme of separation or confinement, as the narrator begins to discuss his salary.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of MIKE, a drug cartel member, his face obscured by a black balaclava and cap, smoking in a dimly lit, decaying room. His male voice (translated from Spanish) explains the logistics of drug trafficking, comparing it to the movement of everyday goods like bananas, oranges, and shoes, emphasizing that "it's a business like any other." He details how smaller independent gangs transport cocaine by speedboat, but the most common and safer method is by container. The camera then shifts to a wider shot of MIKE in silhouette against a frosted glass door, highlighting his anonymity. The scene transitions to an exterior night shot of a beige 'ADUANAS' building with black vehicles, including a military-style truck, parked outside. Inside, a customs officer, later identified by a female narrator as Carlos Pose Suarez, is seen wearing a white face mask and an 'ADUANAS' vest, preparing his uniform in a locker room. Suspenseful music begins as the narrator explains that cartels use speedboats to transport over 280 tons of drugs, including cocaine, monthly across the 14-kilometer strait separating Spain and Morocco. The scene cuts to a harbor at night, showing a large 'ADUANAS' patrol boat docked, with city lights reflecting on the water. The narrator clarifies that Carlos Pose Suarez works for Spanish customs and is preparing for a night patrol to detect smugglers from Morocco on the Strait of Gibraltar. The segment concludes with the customs boat speeding through the dark water, its flag fluttering, leaving a wake, signifying active patrol against the described smuggling operations.

The scene transitions to the interior of a customs facility, where a customs officer, wearing a black vest with 'ADUANAS' and a 'VIGILANCIA ADUANERA' badge, is seen handling a firearm and preparing for duty, as the narrator states that 'Carlos and his colleagues are prepared for anything.' The setting then shifts to the interior of a moving vehicle at night, where Captain Carlos Pose Suarez of the Spanish Civil Guard is seated. He speaks in Spanish, with a calm, authoritative voice, explaining that 'We carry weapons and sometimes we do use them,' though 'It's not frequent, rather rare actually.' Rain streaks the windows, and city lights blur outside, emphasizing the tense, nocturnal atmosphere. He continues, stating that when weapons are used, 'it's only as a deterrent to stop them when they're making very dangerous maneuvers.' The camera briefly focuses on his hand operating a communication device before returning to his face. The narrator concludes by describing this as 'an elaborate and expensive fight,' detailing Spain's security plan against illegal drug smuggling and its increased budget.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens at night with Captain Carlos Pose Suarez, wearing a dark uniform with an 'ADUANAS' badge, speaking in Spanish from inside a vehicle, stating that "safety always comes first" and "staying alive is more important than catching the smugglers," as he drives through a rainy urban environment with city lights blurred. A female narrator then explains that "Police officers estimate that up to 10 speedboats bring drugs ashore here every night," as the scene transitions to a silhouette on a boat gazing at a brightly lit port, followed by a wide shot of the large cargo ship 'ARKAS' docked at an industrial port. Intense, suspenseful music begins to build as the narrator continues, "Two to three times a week they can track down and convict smugglers. Filmed here by the Spanish Civil Guard." The footage shifts to a high-speed pursuit on the open sea, showing a customs patrol boat from a first-person perspective, its engine roaring and water splashing as it navigates rough waters, closing in on a smaller, fast-moving inflatable vessel. The chase culminates with a distinct crash sound as the inflatable boat smashes onto a rocky shoreline, where agents in tactical gear disembark. The narrator then details, "The authorities also often make large cocaine finds in the port of Algeciras. In early 2022, two tons of cocaine were found in a container declared as frozen pike." The scene cuts to a warehouse where officials, including one wearing an 'AGENCIA TRIBUTARIA' vest, are unpacking and inspecting large, plastic-wrapped packages labeled 'MTX 1' and 'DXB'. The final shot shows a customs officer in a dark uniform loading a seized package into the trunk of a black Toyota vehicle at night, as the narrator concludes, "Authorities are primarily concerned with tracking down..." and the music fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens at an industrial port complex under bright sunlight, where two customs officers, identifiable by their high-visibility yellow 'Douane' vests and white hard hats, exit a white Skoda vehicle with yellow and black diagonal stripes. A female narrator explains that for security reasons their names are not revealed, and they are on their way to monitor containers using a brand new procedure that has increased the number of checks tenfold. The officers retrieve a first-aid kit and a black bag from the open trunk before walking purposefully through the port area, flanked by stacks of shipping containers, with the distant sound of a vehicle audible. They navigate between containers, eventually stopping at a large white shipping container, which bears 'CAUTION 9'-6" HIGH' and 'SUPER HEAVY' warning stickers. The narrator clarifies that individual containers are no longer opened; instead, an air sample is taken and secured. Visually, one officer kneels beside the container, using tools to inspect and manipulate the locking mechanism, employing a small device connected by a tube, possibly for sampling, accompanied by a distinct device sound. The narrator adds that later, dogs can efficiently check these samples without being led to each container. The scene then shifts to a close-up of the 'ANONYMOUS Customs officer', who, wearing blue gloves and a radio on his shoulder, speaks directly to the camera in both Dutch and English. He explains that with this technique, it only takes three minutes per container to extract the air, a significant improvement as normally it takes much longer, allowing them to check many more containers. He further notes that this method even works on board ships, where air can also be extracted, and the samples are then taken to a control station.

The scene shifts to a bustling urban street, filled with street sounds, where police officers in high-visibility uniforms stand near a floral memorial, while pedestrians walk by, underscoring a public tragedy. Indoors, ROBIN HOFMANN, a Criminologist at Maastricht University, is introduced by the narrator as someone who 'researches cocaine trafficking' and is 'shocked by the developments right on his doorstep.' He is first seen at a desk, reading an article titled 'How the ‘Mocro Maffia’ are outgunning Dutch police' on a computer screen, which features a photo of a floral memorial, then writing notes. The distinct sound of a wheelchair is heard before he is interviewed in an empty lecture hall. Speaking thoughtfully with expressive hand gestures, ROBIN HOFMANN, in a calm, serious voice with a slight accent, explains that 'the Netherlands is the best example at the moment' of how a drug conflict can 'escalate' and become 'unbelievably brutal' on 'the open street.' segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an ominous, suspenseful electronic score as two silhouetted figures in dark hooded clothing walk down a dimly lit urban street at night, creating a sense of mystery. The scene transitions to a panoramic view of a Berlin city skyline at dusk, featuring illuminated apartment buildings and an industrial chimney, with the iconic Berlin TV Tower glowing in the distance. A calm, serious female voice begins to narrate, stating, "Cocaine is booming. Business is booming. Demand is high," and that "Cocaine is becoming the people's drug." She continues, noting it "still has a good image despite all the media reports about drug cartels and mafia violence." The visual shifts to a bright, serene daytime park where a woman with short blonde hair, wearing a light blue shirt and jeans, walks along a tree-lined path. The female voice reveals, "Addicts like Marie know what a lie this is," as the woman sits on a green park bench, gazing into the sun-dappled greenery. The narrator adds, "it's not always possible to get out. And when you fall, you fall a long way." The camera tilts to the dense canopy of leaves before returning to the woman walking away. A male voice, speaking first in German then translating to English, describes the immense difficulty of "getting off the drug," calling it "more than just a struggle," and "like a war because it's just everywhere." He expresses the hope of never forgetting the hardship so it "never happens to me again." The female voice concludes, "Marie has been clean for a year now," as the music fades, emphasizing the woman's journey from struggle to recovery.

segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins with a tranquil park scene, where a woman with short blonde hair, wearing a light blue button-up shirt and jeans, walks away from the camera along a dirt path lined with trees and benches, suggesting a moment of reflection or peace. The scene transitions to a series of title cards overlaid on urban and aerial shots of Berlin, introducing the documentary 'COCAINE FOR GERMANY: Drug Dealing and Smuggling' by DANEIL SAGER. The credits roll over various cityscapes, including a street under an elevated railway, a busy intersection with a distinctive U-Bahn station, and sweeping aerial views of Berlin’s dense residential and commercial areas. The Berlin TV Tower is visible in the distance during the aerial shots, anchoring the location.
